title: Memory leak causing progressive server slowdown, likely introduced in v2.3 WebSocket notification system
problem: Self-hosted TaskFlow instance experiences a consistent daily memory leak, climbing from ~500MB at startup to 4GB+ by end of day. This causes progressive performance degradation — page loads exceed 10 seconds and API calls time out, requiring a daily server restart. Affects ~200 active users.
root_cause_hypothesis: The v2.3 upgrade introduced an 'improved real-time notifications' feature using WebSockets. The reporter observes an unexpectedly high volume of WebSocket connection and notification dispatch log entries. The most likely cause is that WebSocket connections or their associated resources (event listeners, notification subscriptions, buffers) are not being properly cleaned up when clients disconnect, leading to a cumulative memory leak proportional to connection churn throughout the day.
reproduction_steps: Deploy TaskFlow v2.3 on a server with monitoring (e.g., Grafana tracking memory usage)
Allow normal user activity (~200 users) over the course of a workday
Observe server memory climbing steadily from baseline (~500MB) toward 4GB+
Observe increasing slow-response warnings and request timeouts in server logs
Note the high volume of WebSocket-related log entries (connections, notification dispatches)
environment: TaskFlow v2.3 (upgraded from v2.2 ~1 week ago), Ubuntu 22.04 VM, 8GB RAM, bare metal deployment, PostgreSQL database
impact: All ~200 active users experience degraded performance daily, with the application becoming effectively unusable by late afternoon. Requires manual daily restart as a workaround.
recommended_fix: Investigate the WebSocket notification system introduced/changed in v2.3. Specifically: (1) Check whether WebSocket connections are properly closed and their resources released on client disconnect, navigation, or timeout. (2) Look for event listener or subscription leaks where notification handlers accumulate without cleanup. (3) Compare the WebSocket connection management code between v2.2 and v2.3. (4) Use heap snapshots or memory profiling to confirm whether WebSocket-related objects are the source of the leak.
proposed_test_case: Create a load test that simulates repeated WebSocket connect/disconnect cycles (mimicking users opening and closing the app throughout the day). Monitor server memory over an extended period. Memory should remain stable after connections are closed. Additionally, a unit test should verify that all event listeners and subscriptions are removed when a WebSocket connection is terminated.
information_gaps: Exact runtime version (e.g., Node.js version) — unlikely to change the investigation direction but could matter if the leak is runtime-specific
Exact PostgreSQL version — low relevance since DB connection pooling appears healthy
Whether the issue occurs with fewer users or requires the full 200-user load to manifest
